title: Variations to a Manufacturing or Wholesale Licence
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: An MHRA licence variation keeps your MIA or WDA current. Learn how to classify changes, build defensible evidence and sequence submissions to avoid findings.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury banner shot inside a calm, modern pharmaceutical quality office at the moment a licensed scope is being reviewed. A senior quality professional's hands rest over crisp regulatory documents and an open laptop showing soft, abstract data fields, beside a sleek tablet and a slim folder; in the softly blurred background, a glimpse of a glass-walled cleanroom corridor. The palette is cool teal and cyan against deep navy, lifted by a single subtle green accent on a document tab. Soft natural light rakes from a tall window, shallow depth of field with gentle bokeh, immaculate surfaces, premium and trustworthy mood, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: What is the difference between an MHRA licence variation and a marketing authorisation variation?
faqs.a: A licence variation changes the company authorisation itself — your MIA or WDA — covering things like sites, named QPs or RPs, dosage forms and permitted activities. A marketing authorisation variation, by contrast, changes the product dossier and follows a completely separate classification system. The two are often confused, but they are submitted through different routes and assessed against different criteria.

faqs.q: Can we start a new manufacturing or wholesale activity while the variation is still pending?
faqs.a: No. You must operate strictly within your current authorised scope until the variation is granted. Beginning a new activity, adding a new site, or having personnel act in a named role before approval means operating without the relevant authorisation, which is a serious deficiency that can halt supply. Always plan project timelines around the approval, not the submission, date.

faqs.q: Will an MHRA licence variation trigger an inspection?
faqs.a: It can. Substantive standard variations — such as adding a new sterile operation, a new site or a significant new category of product — may prompt the MHRA to inspect before granting the change. Administrative changes such as a simple company name change usually do not. Build inspection readiness and realistic lead time into your plan whenever the change materially expands what the site is authorised to do.
